title: "Problem with driver for Nvidia FX Go5200"
date: 2008-06-21
forum: Hardware
---

### Post by Daimoneze on 2008-06-21
Using KDE3.

Installing NVIDIA-Linux-x86-173.14.09-pkg1.run causes X to loop. I must then edit xorg.conf and change "nvidia" to "nv" and X will start. Here is the working xorg.conf after this edit:
```
# nvidia-xconfig: X configuration file generated by nvidia-xconfig
# nvidia-xconfig:  version 1.0  (buildmeister@builder63)  Thu Jun  5 00:10:21 PDT 2008

# xorg.conf (X.Org X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "ServerLayout"
    Identifier     "Default Layout"
    Screen         "Default Screen" 0 0
    InputDevice    "Generic Keyboard" "CoreKeyboard"
    InputDevice    "Configured Mouse"
    InputDevice    "Synaptics Touchpad"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Generic Keyboard"
    Driver         "kbd"
    Option         "XkbRules" "xorg"
    Option         "XkbModel" "pc105"
    Option         "XkbLayout" "us"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Configured Mouse"
    Driver         "mouse"
    Option         "CorePointer"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Synaptics Touchpad"
    Driver         "synaptics"
    Option         "SendCoreEvents" "true"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/psaux"
    Option         "Protocol" "auto-dev"
    Option         "HorizEdgeScroll" "0"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "Configured Monitor"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "Configured Video Device"
    Driver         "nv"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Default Screen"
    Device         "Configured Video Device"
    Monitor        "Configured Monitor"
    SubSection     "Display"
        Modes      "nvidia-auto-select"
    EndSubSection
EndSection

```
From what I can tell doing so tells X not to use the driver I just installed and use the default instead. Hardware Drivers Manager does not show any drivers. My main issue seems to be that there is no rendering (openGL) going on. 

I would be glad to supply any additional info. Ideas?

---

### Post by kwirk on 2008-06-21
Id expect this to  say the name of your garphics card:

Section "Device"
    Identifier    [COLOR="Red"] "Configured Video Device"[/COLOR]
    Driver         "nv"
EndSection

Did you run nvidia-xconfig after the driver installation, as this should configure the xorg.conf for your graphics card??

Try lspci and check its listing your graphics card as a device. Did you have the graphics card working before you installed the drivers (using older drivers for example)?

### Post by kwirk on 2008-06-22
Did you follow these instructions before installing the updated drivers.
[http://www.nvnews.net/vbulletin/showthread.php?t=72490](http://www.nvnews.net/vbulletin/showthread.php?t=72490)

I'd disable compiz, and set back to KDE's default window manager and trying the nvidia drivers again. I suspect compiz could be causing your looping.

If it is compiz, try adding some or all of these lines to your xorg.conf

In Section "Screen":
```
    Option         "AddARGBVisuals" "True"
    Option         "AllowGLXWithComposite" "True"
    Option         "AddARGBGLXVisuals" "True"
```

And elsewhere
```
Section "Extensions"
    Option "Composite" "Enable"
EndSection
```

```
Section "Module"
    Load           "glx"
EndSection
```

And give compiz another go.

---

/etc/X11/xorg.conf
# nvidia-xconfig: X configuration file generated by nvidia-xconfig
# nvidia-xconfig:  version 1.0  (buildmeister@builder63)  Thu Jun  5 00:10:21 PDT 2008

# xorg.conf (X.Org X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "ServerLayout"
    Identifier     "Default Layout"
    Screen         "Default Screen" 0 0
    InputDevice    "Generic Keyboard" "CoreKeyboard"
    InputDevice    "Configured Mouse"
    InputDevice    "Synaptics Touchpad"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Generic Keyboard"
    Driver         "kbd"
    Option         "XkbRules" "xorg"
    Option         "XkbModel" "pc105"
    Option         "XkbLayout" "us"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Configured Mouse"
    Driver         "mouse"
    Option         "CorePointer"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Synaptics Touchpad"
    Driver         "synaptics"
    Option         "SendCoreEvents" "true"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/psaux"
    Option         "Protocol" "auto-dev"
    Option         "HorizEdgeScroll" "0"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "Configured Monitor"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "Configured Video Device"
    Driver         "nv"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Default Screen"
    Device         "Configured Video Device"
    Monitor        "Configured Monitor"
    SubSection     "Display"
        Modes      "nvidia-auto-select"
    EndSubSection
EndSection

After the install the looping contiunued. I then reopened xorg.conf and again changed "nvidia" to "nv" under "Configured Video Device" and once again X worked which leads me to the conclusion that it was not in fact compiz.

### Post by kwirk on 2008-06-22
```
   	echo;								\
   	echo "  ERROR: Kernel configuration is invalid.";		\
   	echo "         include/linux/autoconf.h or include/config/auto.conf are mis
   sing.";	\

   .";	\
   	echo;	
```

This doesn't sound right at all. I've had a search around but I'm a bit stuck where to go next. :confused: I'd check you have linux-headers installed. Try posting this problem in the main forums. Hopefully someone more useful will be able to help you. Sorry. :(

### Post by Daimoneze on 2008-06-24
I looked into nvidia-xconfig and realized I didn't have it installed, so I went ahead installed. I then ran it and got:

```
Using X configuration file: "/etc/X11/xorg.conf".
Segmentation fault

```:???:

Very odd indeed. Any ideas how this can be resolved?

### Post by kwirk on 2008-06-25
> **Daimoneze said:**
> I looked into nvidia-xconfig and realized I didn't have it installed, so I went ahead installed. I then ran it and got:

```
Using X configuration file: "/etc/X11/xorg.conf".
Segmentation fault

```:???:

Very odd indeed. Any ideas how this can be resolved?

Had a quick google and...

[http://bugs.debian.org/cgi-bin/bugreport.cgi?bug=429360](http://bugs.debian.org/cgi-bin/bugreport.cgi?bug=429360)

Looks like you've just got to add Module section to your xorg.conf.

### Post by Daimoneze on 2008-07-10
So I decided to scrap what I had and start over with a new Kubuntu KDE4 install. After doing so I did a full update via Adept, downloaded the Nvidia driver from the site (NVIDIA-Linux-x86-173.14.09.pkg1.run for GeForce 5200 FX), and download the build-essential package. The installation of the driver went smoothly and X even started without crashing! Unfortunately, it's not over.

Enabling Desktop Effects in System Settings so I can finally get my precious eye candy causes the display to shift about 1/3 of the way down the display and KWin to crash.

### Post by ptreaster on 2008-08-29
I was searching the forums for the .run extension. I have the NVIDIA Gforece and downloaded the package from Asus. How do I run the package? I am just strating to remember some of the commands, but this is the first .run I've run into.

Thanks!

---

### Post by zot171 on 2009-03-31
hi, i dont know if you ever received answers to some of your questions, so here goes:

.run commands are run using 'sh'
for example:
```
sh NVIDIA-Linux-x86-173.14.09.pkg1.run
```

also, for an older card, such as the go5200 or my fx5500, you may have to use older drivers... i think the 93 drivers are recommended...

if nothing else works you may want to try those

---
